danger_signals.visual_form: Incoming fire patterns from hostile entities
Large mechanical or supercomputer-derived antagonist at stage end
Visual representation in final stage and cutscenes
danger_signals.meaning: Primary existential threat to mankind; narrative antagonist

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental wreckage visible in stage design
danger_signals.meaning: Evidence of prior conflict; hazardous terrain

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items dropped by defeated enemies
Boss defeat triggers stage transition with animated cutscene

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Manifests as automated enemies and mechanical bases throughout stages
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mechanical bases, destroyed infrastructure represent supercomputer's sphere of control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Animated cutscenes between stages explain supercomputer malfunction plot
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game establishes stakes for humanity through voice acting (Sega CD version) and story progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Six distinct stage environments: mechanical bases, destroyed star vessels, Jupiter
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuous enemy waves, escalating boss difficulty, power-up collection mechanics drive forward progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environment transitions from mechanical infrastructure to celestial locations, culminating in supercomputer confrontation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters require targeting weak points; stage environments become more hostile as player approaches antagonist

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final stage presents supercomputer antagonist as primary visual and mechanical focus
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ultimate boss fight with unique attack patterns and weak point targeting

environmental_storytelling.example: Destroyed star vessels visible in stage environments communicate prior casualties and scope of supercomputer threat

environmental_storytelling.technique: Stage environment progression reflects narrative escalation
environmental_storytelling.example: Mechanical bases → destroyed vessels → Jupiter represents player advancing toward supercomputer source
Animated sequences between stages explain plot progression and establish world-state context (enhanced by voice acting in Sega CD version)
Distinct environmental aesthetics for each of six stages create world diversity while maintaining sci-fi mechanical antagonist theme

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to player input; dual cannons fire in configured directions (forward, backward, diagonal); collects power-ups
Approach and fire projectiles at player; drop power-up capsules when destroyed
Exhibit unique attack patterns; possess distinct weak points requiring targeted fire; guard stage progression gates
Serves as final stage antagonist; represents culmination of threat escalation

notes: Sol-Feace communicates world-state primarily through environmental design and narrative cutscenes rather than dynamic faction systems. The visual language is straightforward: mechanical/automated enemies represent supercomputer threat, destroyed environments show prior conflict scope, and stage progression (mechanical bases → star vessels → Jupiter → supercomputer) creates clear narrative geography. Power-up collection provides positive feedback for combat success. Limited faction complexity; essentially binary threat model (player vs. malfunctioning AI). Visual communication relies on sci-fi aesthetic conventions and stage theming rather than complex iconography or faction color schemes.
